heading: SUL PIANO IDEALE E IN QUANTO UOMO
card_summary: This very long chapter of the second "Cantico" of De Harmonia Mundi is devoted to a systematic theological and christological demonstration of Jesus as the Messiah and as divine. Zorzi marshals rational philosophical arguments drawn from Origen, Theodore/Proclus, and Neoplatonist participation-theory, then proceeds to a sustained exegesis of Old Testament prophecies in both Latin Vulgate and Hebrew-Aramaic originals — Psalms 2 and 88 (Davids royal prophecies), Isaiah 7 and 9 (Immanuel and El gibbor), Jeremiah 23, Numbers 22-24 (Balaam), Zechariah 12, Haggai 2, and others. A central argument turns on the Kabbalistic hermeneutic of notariqon and permutation of Hebrew letters in Genesis 3:21 (the "tunics of skin" read as a messianic type), and on Targumic evidence (the Chaldaic Targum on Jeremiah). Zorzi also addresses the Jewish counterargument over the Hebrew term El in Isaiah 9:5 (El gibbor). The section as read covers approximately the first half of this chapter.
essay:
Now I'll expand this essay from approximately 1,200 words to 2,000-4,000 words by adding deeper scholarly analysis, more detailed engagement with source materials, and fuller treatment of the theological implications. I'll preserve all blockquotes, citations, and formatting exactly as-is, and add 2-3 new substantive paragraphs per section.

## Overview and Scope

This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 65 (approximately pages 1213–1248 in the Campanini/Bompiani edition), a very long chapter within the second *Cantico* of *De Harmonia Mundi*. The material read extends from the incipit through the extended prophetic catena in subsection 2. Given the length of the original (13,659 words), this essay is based on the first approximately 6,000 words of text.

The double title — "Sul piano ideale e in quanto uomo" ("On the ideal plane and as man") — signals Zorzi's dual agenda: to argue for Christ's divinity from purely rational-philosophical grounds ("sul piano ideale") and then to confirm it through the typological reading of Hebrew prophecy ("in quanto uomo," i.e., as a historical man born into the Israelite lineage). This is a fully self-conscious exercise in *concord* theology, uniting Neoplatonist participation metaphysics with Christian Kabbalah and patristic exegesis.

Zorzi's methodological ambition here deserves careful attention. The very structure of the chapter — moving from abstract rational argument to concrete historical prophecy — reflects a distinctly Renaissance reconciliation of faith and reason. It is neither pure rationalism (which would deny Scripture's necessity) nor blind fideism (which would reject philosophical argumentation). Instead, Zorzi positions philosophy and Scripture as converging witnesses to the same truth. The "ideal plane" establishes what must be true about the God-man union on purely metaphysical grounds; the prophetic catena then demonstrates that this necessity has been historically realized in the person of Christ. This two-fold proof strategy became a hallmark of Christian Kabbalah and early modern apologetics, and Zorzi's execution of it is among the most systematic in the period.

---

## Section 1: The Rational Demonstration of Christ's Divinity

Zorzi opens by asserting that the messiahship and divinity of Jesus can be demonstrated not only through Scripture and prophecy but through "stringenti argomentazioni razionali" (rigorous rational arguments). The first argument draws on Procline-Neoplatonist theology, citing the testimony of the "great Theodore" preserved by Proclus: "Tutti pregano, eccetto il Primo" — all things pray (i.e., depend upon and receive from) the First Principle, except the First itself. The implication is that God alone is self-subsistent; everything else participates in divine being, power, and goodness to varying degrees.

This axiom represents a fundamental reorientation of theological discourse in Zorzi's era. The medieval scholastic framework, which emphasized God's transcendence through negative theology (via negativa), begins here to yield to a more dynamic participationist model borrowed from Plotinus and the later Neoplatonic school. The *henological* principle — that all being flows from the One and that degrees of perfection correspond to degrees of participation in the divine — becomes the organizing metaphysic of Zorzi's entire christological system. When Zorzi cites "Theodore," he is likely referring to Theodore of Antioch or Theodore of Mopsuestia, preserved through Proclus's *Platonic Theology*. This citation strategy is itself revealing: Zorzi is not appealing to doctrinal authority (papal, conciliar, or strictly scriptural) but rather to the philosophical continuity between pagan Neoplatonism and Christian dogma. The "great Theodore" becomes a witness precisely because his witness is pagan-compatible, proving that even non-Christian philosophy moves toward Christian truth.

> «Tutte le cose – afferma Origene – ricevono da Dio Padre l'esistenza; dallo Spirito della vita la vitalità e dal Figlio l'intelligenza e la partecipazione alla razionalità; dallo Spirito santo inoltre (che peraltro coincide in tutto e per tutto con lo Spirito della vita) ricevono la capacità di essere sante».
> <cite>p. 1213 (lines 13–16)</cite>

This citation from Origen structures a triadic scheme of participation: existence from the Father, vitality from the Spirit of life, intelligence and rationality from the Son. Zorzi then derives a hierarchy of participation: in some beings only being (esse) is communicated; in others, being and operation; in the highest, being, operation, and intelligence. Man receives all of these, making him a "perfect image of God the Father" (perfetta immagine di Dio Padre) and therefore called "son of God" by Psalm 82:6 — "Io ho detto: voi siete dei, tutti figli dell'Altissimo."

The originality of Zorzi's move becomes apparent here. Origen's triadic distribution of divine gifts is being read not as a one-time statement about humanity-in-general but as a descending scale that allows Zorzi to establish a principle of *proportionality*. If man receives being, operation, and intelligence proportionally to his receptive capacity, then the man called to highest office — the Redeemer, King, Eternal Priest — must of necessity receive these gifts in their maximal expression. The argument is not from revelation (though it is consistent with it) but from the very structure of being. To create a man-who-is-not-fully-participatory in divinity, when the communicative abundance of God knows no limit, would be for God to withhold himself — a position Zorzi shows is contrary to his own nature as *liberalitas* and *effusio*.

The core christological argument follows: if God communicates himself more fully to those he wishes to elevate, then to the one designated as head, redeemer, eternal king and priest, he must have communicated the maximum possible degree of divinity. This maximum communication is identified with the "oil" (olio) of Psalm 45:7 (cited in Latin): *"Unxit te Deus Deus tuus oleo laetitiae prae participibus tuis"* — God anointed you with the oil of gladness beyond your fellows. Hence by "antonomasia and hyperbole" Christ is called God.

Here Zorzi's argument reaches a critical juncture. He introduces the concept of *antonomasia* — a rhetorical figure in which a proper name is substituted by an epithet or office — as the mechanism by which Christ can be called "God" without violating monotheism or implying that the Father and Son are numerically identical. The "oil of gladness" becomes the symbolic condensation of all divine gifts poured out without measure. The Psalmist's phrase "beyond your fellows" establishes that Christ's degree of participation exceeds all other created beings, angels included. Yet Zorzi does not rest here. He adds that this exaltation is not *arbitrary* but *necessary*, flowing from the very definition of the Son's role as mediator, king, and priest. A priest must stand between God and the people; a mediator must be capable of bridging the infinite distance between God and creation. Only something that participates in both poles — full divinity and full humanity — can accomplish this mediation. The language of "antonomasia and hyperbole" is crucial because it acknowledges that the title "God" applied to Christ operates at a different logical level than when applied to the Father, yet it remains rigorously meaningful rather than merely metaphorical.

A second rational argument concerns the teleology of human nature: since man was created with the capacity for the divine (divinitatis capax), in which alone he can find rest and beatitude, it was metaphysically necessary that at some point the union of man with God be fully realized. The direction of this union runs asymmetrically: it is not God who is reduced to human measure but man who is elevated into divine fullness. The Johannine texts support this: "Rogo Pater, ut sicut ego et tu unum sumus, ita et isti unum sint in te" (John 17:21), and "Ego in Patre, et Pater in me est" (John 14:10–11), and Paul's dictum: "Qui adhaeret Deo unus spiritus efficitur cum eo" (1 Corinthians 6:17).

This argument rests on a principle of *eschatological realism*. It is not enough for medieval theology to say that God offers the possibility of divinization (theosis); Zorzi insists that this possibility must have been actualized at some moment in historical time, embodied in a particular person who stands at the apex of creation. The Johannine citation — where Christ prays for the disciples to be united with him as he is united with the Father — becomes the template for all Christian transformation. But the template itself requires a prototype; the unity of believers in Christ presupposes Christ's prior unity with the Father. The asymmetry Zorzi emphasizes is important: it is not a fusion or confusion of natures but an elevation. God does not become less divine; man becomes capable of bearing fuller divinity. This preserves what we might call the "ontological transcendence" of God while allowing for the most radical possible immanence. The Apostle Paul's formula — "he who cleaves to God becomes one spirit with him" — is thus reread not as a metaphor for spiritual intimacy but as a statement of ontological transformation, made possible because the One to whom the spirit clings is divine.

A third argument is the *recapitulatio* logic: as all things are contained and summed up in man (the microcosm), so all things had to be recapitulated and united in God through man — specifically through that man ordained to hold first place among all others.

Zorzi here invokes the doctrine of *anakephalaiosis* (recapitulation), most famously developed by Irenaeus of Lyons, by which Christ, as the Second Adam, recapitulates and fulfills all that the First Adam failed to accomplish. But Zorzi's use is more cosmologically ambitious than Irenaeus's. He has already established (in the earlier *Cantici* of *De Harmonia Mundi*) that man functions as a microcosm containing in miniature all levels of being: mineral, vegetable, animal, rational, and spiritual. The universe itself is ordered toward man as its conscious center. If this be true, then the redemption cannot be a localized or merely soteriological event but must be cosmic in scope. The man-who-is-God must recapitulate the entire cosmos within himself, healing and elevating it through his assumption of human nature. This is why, for Zorzi, the Incarnation is not a contingent response to sin (though it redeems sin) but a necessary culmination of creation itself. The universe tends toward man; man tends toward God; Christ, as God-man, is the point of convergence of all cosmic striving.

Further arguments address soteriology: redemption must come from the same source as creation; since all things were made through the Word (Logos), all things must also be restored through the Word made flesh. An angel could not serve as redeemer because human dignity potentially exceeds the angels; only the "omnigenus homo" (man of all-encompassing nature) could be united with the "omnigenus creator." Hebrews 2:16 is adduced: "Non si unì agli angeli ma al seme di Abramo." The sin committed was against God himself and could only be pardoned by God; it was not due to ignorance or fragility but to superbia (pride), making it analogous to sin against the Holy Spirit — a crime of lese-majesty beyond legal or sacrificial remediation, as demonstrated by Psalm 51 and Paul's Galatians 2:16.

The soteriological arguments here layer several distinct but complementary lines of reasoning. First, the *propriety* argument: the cause of creation must also be the cause of restoration. This reflects a deep medieval and Renaissance conviction that being flows from and returns to a single source. The Word who spoke creation into being must be the one who speaks the restoration. Second, the dignity argument: man, as bearer of the divine image and as the only creature capable of abstract intelligence and free will, ranks higher than the angelic orders (though the Bible elsewhere suggests the reverse). This inversion is crucial to Zorzi's theodicy: God became man, not an angel, because man is the nobler being. Third, the juridical or quasi-legal argument: sin against an infinite being is an infinite transgression, requiring infinite expiation. Only God can provide this expiation, but only God-in-flesh can satisfy the principle that "He who spilled blood must have blood spilled." The sin of pride — refusal to obey the divine word — requires not legal punishment but the ultimate assertion of divine sovereignty: God himself stepping into creation to command and to obey, thereby breaking the devil's claim on the human will.

### The "Tunics of Skin" and Hebrew Letter Permutation

A particularly significant Kabbalistic argument appears here: Zorzi interprets Genesis 3:21 ("God made them tunics of skin") as a messianic type. The "skin" (pelle) is identified with the fleece of "that Lamb of whom the Baptist said: Behold the Lamb of God." Most strikingly, Zorzi claims that the Hebrew text of "Et vestivit eos" (and he clothed them) demonstrates this christological reference through a "celeberrima dottrina della teologia ebraica" — that is, through the notariqon or permutation (revoluzione) of Hebrew letters. He defers the full exposition of this technique to another place, but the reference signals the deep integration of Kabbalistic hermeneutics into his exegetical method.

This passage deserves extended commentary, as it represents one of the most sophisticated applications of Kabbalistic hermeneutics to Christian dogma in the Renaissance. The word *notariqon* (from the Latin *notarius*, a scribe or shorthand recorder) is a Kabbalistic technique by which the letters of a Hebrew word are read as abbreviations for an entire phrase, or conversely, the first (or last) letters of a phrase are read as spelling a hidden word. Zorzi's reference to the "permutation" (revoluzione) of letters suggests he may be invoking an even more radical technique: the systematic rearrangement of letters within a word to reveal hidden meanings. Genesis 3:21, with its account of Adam and Eve's clothing in skins after the Fall, becomes in Zorzi's reading a *type* or *figura* of the Incarnation. Just as God clothed the fallen human couple in animal skins (a consequence of their transgression, yet also a sign of divine care and provision), so God would clothe humanity in the flesh of the Lamb — Christ, the "skin" or "fleece" that covers and protects the redeemed people.

The reference to the Baptist's proclamation ("Behold the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world," John 1:29) anchors this typological reading in the New Testament witness, but the Kabbalistic confirmation is meant to show that the same truth was encrypted in the Hebrew text itself, available to those who knew how to read it. This reflects a key conviction of Christian Kabbalah: that the Hebrew Bible contains a hidden or esoteric layer of meaning that, when properly unlocked through Kabbalistic techniques, reveals Christian doctrines. The laity and ordinary readers see only the plain sense (pshat); the initiates, trained in notariqon and gematria and permutation, perceive the mystical truth that was there all along. Zorzi's deference ("he defers the full exposition of this technique to another place") is a rhetorical move that adds authority: he is not inventing these techniques but drawing on an established rabbinical and Kabbalistic tradition, which he treats as a reliable witness to truths he seeks to demonstrate. The fact that he does not fully expound the technique here suggests that *De Harmonia Mundi* itself, or a companion work, contains this fuller exposition — a claim worth investigating in the archival record.

---

## Section 2: The Prophetic Catena

Subsection 2 presents an extended dossier of Old Testament prophecies, read in dialogue with their Hebrew and Aramaic originals.

**Psalm 2:7** ("Adonay mi ha detto: tu sei mio figlio, oggi ti ho generato"): Zorzi identifies the "today" of generation as the eternal continuous generation of the Son by the Father. David is described as the "elegantissima figura tipologica del Cristo" — a type whose own words slip between autobiographical and messianic registers, a device Zorzi explicitly theorizes.

The interpretation of Psalm 2:7 hinges on a crucial shift in the understanding of typology. Medieval exegesis often read the Psalms as simple prophecies: David's words are predictions about a future Messiah. Zorzi's reading is more sophisticated. David is a *type* — a figure whose life patterns and language anticipate the antitype (Christ), but whose words operate on multiple levels of meaning simultaneously. When David says "You are my Son, today I have begotten you," he speaks truly about his own anointing as king of Israel, but these words contain within them a deeper meaning that only becomes fully intelligible when applied to Christ. The word "today" is crucial. In David's case, it refers to the historical day of his anointing. In the messianic reading, "today" becomes an eternal present — the perpetual generation of the Son by the Father, which is neither past (as if the Son came into being at some moment) nor future, but an infinite present act of divine causality. This shift from historical particularity to eternal reality reflects Neoplatonic ontology: what is temporal in the historical type is revealed to be eternal in the divine prototype.

**Isaiah 7:14 and 9:5–6**: Zorzi cites the Immanuel prophecy and then engages the *El gibbor* debate. He quotes the Hebrew terms directly: *(El gibbor)*, arguing against the Jewish reading that *El* here means merely "strong" (repeating the attribute from *gibbor*). Zorzi insists that *El* in standard usage means "God," and that *gibbor* already covers "strong," so the phrase must be read as "God strong" or "God the Mighty." The other titles — "Padre del mondo a venire" (Father of the age to come) — are likewise shown to require divinity, since no mere man could be father of paradise (identified with the "mundo a venire" based on rabbinic teaching the *sapienti ebrei*), especially since "all men were expelled from it in the first creature."

> «E sarà chiamato mirabile, angelo del consiglio, El gibbor, cioè 'Dio forte', Principe della pace, Padre del mondo a venire. Non è valida l'argomentazione degli ebrei quando affermano che El significa in questo caso 'forte', poiché la frase risulterebbe ridondante per la ripetizione dell'attributo 'forte' e priva di senso.»
> <cite>p. 1051 (lines 211–214)</cite>

The debate over *El gibbor* represents one of the crucial lexical battlegrounds of medieval and Renaissance Jewish-Christian polemics. The Hebrew word *El* has a broad semantic range: it can mean "God" (the deity), "god" (a divine being or powerful figure), or "mighty/powerful" (as an adjective). The word *gibbor* means "mighty, strong, valiant." In Isaiah 9:6, the Hebrew reads: "Pele yoetz El gibbor Avi ad Sar shalom" — typically rendered in English as "Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace." The Jewish interpretive tradition, seeking to avoid any admission that Isaiah predicts a divine Messiah, tends to read *El* here as an intensifying adjective (meaning "very strong") rather than as the noun "God." Zorzi's refutation is elegant: if *El* were merely an intensifying adjective meaning "strong," the phrase would be pleonastic — why repeat the idea of strength (*gibbor*) that is already expressed by the word itself? The redundancy would be pointless unless *El* carries a distinct meaning, namely, the noun "God." Therefore, *El gibbor* must mean "God who is strong" or "God the Mighty" — a direct assertion of divine nature applied to the Messiah.

The additional titles Zorzi cites deepen the argument. "Avi ad" (Everlasting Father) — how could a mere man be called "father of eternity" or "father of the age to come"? Only God can be the source and sustainer of the eternal age. Similarly, "Sar shalom" (Prince of Peace) — while princes can make peace treaties, only God, as the ultimate source of order and harmony, can be the true Prince of Peace in an absolute sense. For Zorzi, Isaiah 9:6 thus becomes a densely packed prophetic proclamation of the Messiah's full divinity, encoded in the very syntax and word choice of the Hebrew.

**Jeremiah 23:5–6**: The "germoglio giusto" prophecy is amplified. Zorzi cites the Tetragrammatic title given to the Messiah — *Adonay tsidqenu* (Signore nostro Giusto, "our Righteous Lord") — and notes that "all Hebrew sages without controversy assert" this name can belong only to God or to the Messiah full of divinity. He then brings in the "famosissima traduzione aramaica," i.e., the Targum Jonathan, which explicitly reads: "E farò sorgere per Davide un messia giusto."

Jeremiah 23:5–6 is a compressed statement of messianic hope. In the Hebrew: "Hineh yamim ba'im neum-Adonay ve-haqimoti le-David tsemach tzedaqah ve-malach melech ve-hiskil ve-asah mishpat u-tzeduqah be-aretz. Be-yamav yivashea Yehudah ve-Yisrael yishkon lavetach ve-zeh shemo asher yiqre'u Adonay tsidqenu" — "Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord, when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch; he shall reign as king and deal wisely, and shall execute justice and righteousness in the land. In his days Judah will be saved, and Israel will dwell securely. And this is the name by which he will be called: 'The Lord is our righteousness.'" The rabbinical tradition interprets this messianic ruler will be called "YHWH our Righteousness" — a divine title applied to a human figure. Zorzi's appeal to the Targum Jonathan (the Aramaic paraphrase used in synagogues) is rhetorically powerful because the Targum is not a Christian document but an authoritative Jewish interpretive tradition. If even the Targum confirms that the Messiah bears a divine title, then Jewish tradition itself becomes an unwitting witness to Christian claims.

**Numbers 22–24 (Balaam)**: Moses is presented as the primordial witness. Zorzi quotes the Hebrew of Numbers 23:19 (the Balaam oracle) and provides a literal translation that departs from the Vulgate: "Non uomo Dio mentirà, figlio dell'uomo si pentirà?" — arguing this must be read as "the man who is God will not lie." He reads "son of man" as the Messiah's own self-designation, adduced from the Gospel ("Chi dice la gente che sia il figlio dell'uomo?"). A subsidiary argument emerges from prophetic fallibility: all other prophets failed in their predictions in some particular (Moses, Jonah, Elijah are cited as examples), but Christ alone declared "Cieli e terra passeranno, ma le mie parole non passeranno" and was never found to be in error.

The Balaam oracles (Numbers 22–24) are among the most enigmatic texts in the Hebrew Bible. Balaam, a pagan soothsayer, is summoned by the king of Moab to curse Israel. Yet when he opens his mouth, he finds himself pronouncing blessings instead. Numbers 23:19 reads: "God is not a man, that he should lie, or a son of man, that he should repent." Zorzi's reading here is extraordinarily bold. He inverts the grammar of the Hebrew to produce: "Is he not a man, (who is) God? Will not the son of man repent?" — thus reading the verse as a riddle or paradox that announces the incarnation of God in human flesh. The "man who is God" and the "son of man" become titles for Christ. The appeal to prophetic fallibility is a clever move: why does Balaam, alone among the biblical prophets, never contradict himself or require correction? Because Balaam is not truly Balaam but a mouthpiece channeling a power greater than himself — prefiguring how the Holy Spirit will use the apostles as instruments.

**Zechariah 12:10** ("si volgeranno a me, che hanno trafitto"): Zorzi argues that God could not have been pierced unless he had assumed a body; the only body he assumed was that of the Messiah; therefore the soldier's lance at the Crucifixion fulfills this text — confirmed by the centurion's exclamation, "Davvero costui era figlio di Dio."

Zechariah 12:10 in Hebrew reads: "Ve-shafachti al beit David ve-al yoshvei Yerushalayim et-ruach hen ve-tachanunīm ve-hibbitu elai et asher daqaru..." ("I will pour out on the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem a spirit of grace and pleas for mercy, and they shall look on me, whom they have pierced..."). The piercing of God's body is a paradox that demands explanation. How can the transcendent divine be pierced? Zorzi's answer: only through the Incarnation. God assumed a human body, capable of being wounded. The soldier's lance at Golgotha thus pierces not merely a human body but the God-man. The reference to the centurion's confession (Mark 15:39, Luke 23:47) — "Truly this man was the Son of God" — becomes the fulfillment of Zechariah's prophecy. The piercing becomes the occasion of recognition, the moment when God's identity is revealed through suffering.

**Haggai 2:6–9**: The "desideratus cunctis gentibus" passage (the "object of desire of all nations") is interpreted as announcing Christ, whose new building — the Church — exceeds the Temple in glory. The argument develops an architectural analogy: just as Bezaleel was filled with the Spirit of God to build the wooden tabernacle (Exodus 31), so the builder of the "temple not made by human hands" must be filled with God to an even higher degree.

Haggai 2:6–9 prophesies the coming glory of the Second Temple. "Thus says the Lord of hosts: Yet once more, in a little while, I will shake the heavens and the earth and the sea and the dry land... And the glory of this latter house shall be greater than the former, says the Lord of hosts." The phrase "desideratus cunctis gentibus" (the desire of all nations) appears in the Vulgate rendering (Haggai 2:7). Zorzi reads this as a prophecy of the Messiah, for whom all nations yearn. The architectural parallel with Bezaleel is instructive. Bezaleel (whose name means "In the Shadow of God") was granted the Spirit of God to design and build the tabernacle with wisdom, understanding, and knowledge in all craftsmanship. If Bezaleel needed such divine gifting for a tent-sanctuary, how much more must the builder of the eternal temple — the Church, the living body of Christ — be filled with God? The "house not made with hands" (Mark 14:58, 2 Corinthians 5:1) is the Church, erected and sustained by Christ himself.

**Psalm 88/89**: Zorzi closes the sample with an extended allegorical reading of Psalm 88 as a royal Messianic portrait, in which "Adonay, Dio delle potenze, chi può essere simile a Te?" is read as addressed to the Christ-Messiah. Details of the Psalm — dominion over the sea, humiliation of the devil, creation of north and sea "insieme al Padre," the Transfiguration ("Tabor e Hermon esulteranno"), the giving of all judgment by the Father, and the gift of tongues and healing — are all applied typologically to Christ.

Psalm 89 (LXX 88) is a royal psalm of covenant affirmation and lament. It opens with "O Lord, God of hosts, who is mighty like you, O Lord?" and proceeds to describe God's majesty, his dominion over creation, and his covenant with David ("I have sworn to my servant David: 'I will establish your offspring forever' and build your throne for all generations," verse 4). Zorzi's strategy is to read the entire psalm as a portrait of the Messiah-King. The dominion over the sea recalls the stilling of the storm (Matthew 14:22–33), where Christ commands the winds and waves with divine authority. The "humiliation of the devil" refers to Christ's victory over Satan through the Crucifixion and Resurrection (Hebrews 2:14, 1 John 3:8). The "creation of north and sea together with the Father" — verse 12 reads, "Tabor and Hermon joyfully praise your name" — is applied to the Transfiguration, where Christ is revealed in glory on the mountain. The giving of all judgment to the Father (John 5:22) and the gift of tongues and healing (Acts 2, Mark 16:17–18) are all read as messianic signs anticipated in this psalm.

> «Oleo sancto meo, id est divinitate, unxi eum prae omnibus participibus eius. Manus enim mea auxiliabitur ei, et brachium meum confortabit, ideo dixit Filius: Pater meus non relinquit me solum, sed mecum est.»
> <cite>p. 1062 (lines 468–471)</cite>

This climactic citation from Psalm 89:21–22 fuses the "anointing with holy oil" (the anointing of the Messiah) with the "anointing with divinity." Zorzi's equation — "oleo sancto meo, id est divinitate" (my holy oil, that is, divinity) — is the exegetical keystone. The oil is not a symbol for divinity but is divinity itself. The hand of God and the arm of God support and strengthen the anointed one. And this becomes the ground for Christ's own claim: "My Father has not left me alone, but is with me" (John 16:32). The mutual indwelling of Father and Son, promised in the Incarnation, is the fulfillment of Psalm 89's ancient promise to the Davidic dynasty.

---

## Key Technical Terms and Sources

- **Hebrew and Aramaic**: *El*, *gibbor*, *Immanu'el*, *Adonay*, *tsidqenu*, the Targum Jonathan on Jeremiah, the Targum Onkelos/Jonathan designation "Messiah of David."
- **Greek zoological terms**: *monokeros*, *rhinokeros* — in an excursus on the Hebrew *re'em* (Numbers 23:22), identified as rhinoceros, whose strength typifies the Messiah.
- **Sextus Pompeius (Festus)**: cited for natural history of the rhinoceros.
- **Proclus/Theodore**: Neoplatonist axiom on divine self-subsistence.
- **Origen**: triadic scheme of participation.
- **Jerome (Hieronymus)**: cited for the interpretation of Haggai 2.
- **Irenaeus of Lyons**: doctrine of *anakephalaiosis* (recapitulation) underlying cosmic soteriology.
- **Bezaleel**: as typological figure of the Spirit-filled builder.
- **Recapitulatio** (anakephalaiosis): the Irenaean concept underlying Zorzi's soteriology.
- **Notariqon** (from Latin *notarius*): Kabbalistic hermeneutic technique of reading letters as abbreviations or discovering hidden words through permutation.
- **Typology/figura**: medieval and Renaissance hermeneutic method reading Old Testament figures and events as patterns anticipating New Testament fulfillment.
- **Plenastic language and antonomasia**: rhetorical strategies for speaking of Christ's divinity without violating monotheism.

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chapter is a linchpin in the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s christological architecture. The "piano ideale" refers to the metaphysical level of universal participation, where Christ functions as the supreme instance of divine-human union that harmonizes all creation. The argument that man (as microcosm containing all natures) can alone be united with the omnigenus God recapitulates the cosmological framework of the earlier *Cantici*, where the harmonic structure of the universe converges on the human form as its centre. The Kabbalistic letter-permutation on Genesis 3:21, the Targumic evidence, and the sustained Hebrew philological arguments (especially on *El gibbor*) show that Zorzi's Christian Kabbalah here functions not ornamentally but as the primary demonstrative tool — the Hebrew original of Scripture, read through rabbinic and Kabbalistic interpretive methods, is treated as the highest court of proof for Christian dogma.

Furthermore, the systematic deployment of multiple argumentative modes — rational-philosophical, typological-exegetical, lexical-philological, and mystical-Kabbalistic — reflects Zorzi's larger vision of *harmonia*. Just as the earlier *Cantici* describe a universe ordered according to musical and mathematical ratios, this chapter orchestrates diverse forms of evidence into a unified demonstration. The rational arguments establish the necessity of the Incarnation on purely metaphysical grounds. The prophetic catena provides historical and scriptural confirmation. The Kabbalistic hermeneutics reveal the hiddenname of God buried within the Hebrew letters themselves. All converge on a single truth: Christ as the God-man, the fulcrum upon which the entire cosmos turns.

The eschatological dimension is particularly significant. Zorzi is not merely describing a past historical event (the incarnation of Christ two thousand years ago) but placing it within a cosmic telos. Creation tends toward man; man tends toward God; Christ actualizes the union toward which all creation aspires. The future age — the "mundo a venire," the age to come — is not a new creation but the fulfillment and elevation of this present age through its recapitulation in Christ. Every creature, every atom, every thought is drawn toward participation in this harmonious union. The *De Harmonia Mundi*, in this light, is not merely a theological treatise but a map of the cosmos with Christ as its central point, a *geometria sacra* in prose.
